sites.name: Wawel Royal Castle
sites.description: The historic seat of Polish kings for over 500 years, this hilltop complex above the Vistula River combines Gothic, Renaissance and Baroque architecture. The State Rooms, Royal Private Apartments, Crown Treasury and Armoury display centuries of royal heritage. Beneath the hill, the legendary Wawel Dragon (Smok Wawelski) is said to have once terrorized Kraków until a clever cobbler defeated it, commemorated today by a fire-breathing bronze sculpture by the riverbank.
sites.seasonal_notes: Castle interiors are open year-round. Summer brings the longest hours and the busiest crowds. The courtyards and dragon statue are pleasant in spring and autumn; winter offers a quieter, atmospheric visit.
sites.booking_tips: Buy timed tickets online in advance, as daily numbers for the State Rooms and exhibitions are limited. Each section (Crown Treasury, Cathedral, Dragon's Den) has a separate ticket.

sites.name: Wawel Cathedral
sites.description: The coronation and burial church of Polish monarchs, standing within the Wawel complex. This Gothic cathedral with later additions holds royal tombs, the Sigismund Chapel (a Renaissance masterpiece), and the great Sigismund Bell of 1520. The crypts shelter kings, queens, national poets and statesmen, making it the spiritual heart of the Polish nation.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round with reduced visiting on Sundays and during services. The bell tower climb is best in clear weather for views over the city.
sites.booking_tips: Tickets are sold at the house opposite the cathedral entrance and cover the royal tombs, the Sigismund Bell tower and the museum. Climbing to the bell involves steep wooden stairs.

sites.name: Main Market Square (Rynek Główny)
sites.description: The largest medieval market square in Europe, laid out in 1257 and at the center of Kraków's UNESCO-listed Old Town. Ringed by townhouses, churches and cafes, it is dominated by the Cloth Hall and St. Mary's Basilica. Street performers, flower stalls, horse-drawn carriages and the hourly hejnał trumpet call give the square its constant life.
sites.seasonal_notes: The square hosts the famous Christmas Market from late November through December and Easter markets in spring. Summer fills the outdoor cafes; early mornings any time of year are calm and photogenic.
sites.booking_tips: Free to wander. Visit at the top of the hour to hear the hejnał from St. Mary's tower. Cafe terraces facing the square charge a premium.

sites.name: St. Mary's Basilica (Kościół Mariacki)
sites.description: A towering Gothic brick church on the Main Market Square, famous for its uneven twin towers and its hourly hejnał, a trumpet call played from the taller tower toward the four cardinal directions. The melody breaks off abruptly mid-note in memory of a medieval trumpeter said to have been shot in the throat while warning the city of a Mongol attack. Inside is the magnificent late-Gothic wooden altarpiece carved by Veit Stoss between 1477 and 1489.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open to visitors outside Mass times year-round. The carved altarpiece is unveiled daily in a short ceremony. Tower climbs operate in the warmer months.
sites.booking_tips: Tourists enter through the side entrance with a ticket bought from the office across the square; worshippers use the main door free of charge. Time your visit for the unveiling of the Veit Stoss altarpiece.

sites.name: Cloth Hall (Sukiennice)
sites.description: A Renaissance trading hall running down the middle of the Main Market Square, a center of Kraków's commerce since the Middle Ages. The ground-floor arcade still houses stalls selling amber, woodcraft and souvenirs, while the upper floor holds the National Museum's Gallery of 19th-Century Polish Art with works by Matejko and other masters.
sites.seasonal_notes: The arcade shops operate year-round and shelter visitors in poor weather. The art gallery upstairs is open most days except Mondays.
sites.booking_tips: Browsing the arcade is free; the upstairs gallery requires a separate National Museum ticket, free on certain days of the week. Bargaining at the stalls is uncommon.

sites.name: Rynek Underground Museum
sites.description: An award-winning archaeological museum beneath the Main Market Square, revealing the medieval streets, market stalls and trade routes uncovered during excavations. Glass floors, holograms and multimedia displays bring Kraków's merchant past to life four meters below the modern square.
sites.seasonal_notes: A perfect rainy-day or hot-summer refuge, kept cool underground year-round. Closed on the second Monday of each month.
sites.booking_tips: Book online in advance, as the underground spaces have limited capacity and sell out in high season. The entrance is on the Cloth Hall side of the square.

sites.name: St. Florian's Gate and Barbican
sites.description: The last surviving city gate of Kraków's medieval walls and the adjoining Barbican, a round fortified outpost from around 1499 that is one of the best-preserved of its kind in Europe. Together they mark the start of the Royal Route into the Old Town, and the walls nearby double as an open-air gallery for local painters.
sites.seasonal_notes: The gate and outer walls can be admired any time year-round. The Barbican interior opens seasonally, mainly in the warmer months.
sites.booking_tips: Passing through the gate is free. A combined ticket covers the Barbican and the surviving stretch of city walls. Best paired with a walk down Floriańska Street.

sites.name: Collegium Maius
sites.description: The oldest building of the Jagiellonian University, founded in 1364, and one of the oldest university structures in the world. Its arcaded Gothic courtyard, lecture halls and treasury of historic scientific instruments recall students such as Nicolaus Copernicus. A musical clock in the courtyard plays figures on the hour.
sites.seasonal_notes: Guided museum tours run year-round, with the courtyard freely accessible by day. Demand is higher in summer and around the academic year.
sites.booking_tips: Interior visits are by guided tour only and should be reserved ahead, especially for English-language slots. The courtyard and clock can be seen without a ticket.

sites.name: Kazimierz
sites.description: Kraków's former Jewish quarter, a once-independent town now famed for its layered history and lively atmosphere. Cobbled streets hold historic synagogues, the Remuh and its old cemetery, and the bustling Plac Nowy market with its round food hall. By night the district's bars and cafes make it the heart of Kraków's social scene.
sites.seasonal_notes: Atmospheric year-round; the Jewish Culture Festival animates the quarter in late June and early July. Summer evenings spill out onto the squares, while winter is cozy and candlelit.
sites.booking_tips: Free to explore on foot. Individual synagogues charge small entry fees and most close on Saturdays. A guided walking tour adds valuable historical context.

sites.name: Oskar Schindler's Enamel Factory
sites.description: The former enamelware factory in Podgórze where Oskar Schindler employed and saved more than 1,000 Jewish workers during the German occupation. Now a branch of the Museum of Kraków, its immersive exhibition tells the story of the city under Nazi rule from 1939 to 1945 through reconstructed streets, documents and personal testimonies.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round and a strong choice in any weather. Free-admission days draw very long queues; closed on the first Tuesday of each month.
sites.booking_tips: Book timed tickets online well ahead, as this is one of Kraków's most popular museums and frequently sells out. Allow at least 90 minutes for the dense exhibition.

sites.name: Ghetto Heroes' Square (Plac Bohaterów Getta)
sites.description: A poignant memorial square in Podgórze marking the site where the Kraków Ghetto's Jewish residents were assembled before deportation. The square is filled with rows of empty steel-and-bronze chairs, a haunting installation symbolizing the furniture left behind during the 1943 liquidation of the ghetto.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open and accessible year-round as a public square. A reflective stop in any season, often visited alongside the Schindler Factory and the Pharmacy Under the Eagle museum.
sites.booking_tips: Free to visit at any time. The adjacent Eagle Pharmacy (Apteka pod Orłem) museum requires a ticket and adds context about the ghetto.

sites.name: Galicia Jewish Museum
sites.description: A museum in Kazimierz commemorating the Jewish heritage and the Holocaust in the historic region of Galicia. Its permanent photographic exhibition documents traces of Jewish life and destruction across southern Poland, complemented by changing exhibitions, lectures and a bookshop.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round, providing an indoor cultural visit in any weather. Special events and temporary exhibitions run throughout the year.
sites.booking_tips: Tickets are available on arrival; no advance booking is usually needed. Combine with a Kazimierz walking tour for fuller context.

sites.name: Czartoryski Museum
sites.description: One of Poland's oldest museums, home to Leonardo da Vinci's celebrated portrait 'Lady with an Ermine'. The recently restored galleries display an exceptional collection of European paintings, decorative arts and antiquities assembled by the Czartoryski family, with the Leonardo as its undisputed centerpiece.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round except Mondays; an excellent indoor option in any season. Free-admission days are busy around the Leonardo gallery.
sites.booking_tips: Reserve tickets online to guarantee entry to the busy 'Lady with an Ermine' room. The museum is part of the National Museum in Kraków.

sites.name: Saints Peter and Paul Church
sites.description: The first Baroque church in Kraków, built for the Jesuits in the early 17th century and lined with statues of the twelve apostles along its facade railing. Inside, a Foucault pendulum demonstrates the rotation of the Earth, and the church hosts regular classical music concerts beneath its grand dome.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round outside services. Foucault pendulum demonstrations are held on selected days; evening concerts run throughout the year.
sites.booking_tips: Daytime visits are typically free or by small donation; concerts and pendulum demonstrations require a ticket. Located on Grodzka Street along the Royal Route.

sites.name: Planty Park
sites.description: A green ring of parkland encircling the Old Town, created in the early 19th century on the site of the demolished medieval walls and moat. Its tree-lined paths, benches and monuments make a pleasant loop linking the city's major sights, popular with strolling locals and visitors alike.
sites.seasonal_notes: Lush and shaded in spring and summer, golden in autumn, and quiet under snow in winter. A relaxing route year-round between Old Town attractions.
sites.booking_tips: Free and open at all times. The full loop circling the Old Town takes under an hour at a leisurely pace.

sites.name: Kościuszko Mound (Kopiec Kościuszki)
sites.description: An earthen mound raised in the 1820s in honor of national hero Tadeusz Kościuszko, ringed by a 19th-century fortress. A spiral path climbs to the summit, which offers sweeping panoramic views over Kraków, the Vistula valley and, on clear days, the distant Tatra Mountains.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round; clear days reward visitors with the longest views. The climb can be muddy after rain or icy in winter, so sturdy footwear helps.
sites.booking_tips: A ticket covers the mound climb and the small museum in the surrounding fort. It sits west of the center; reachable by tram and a short uphill walk or bus.

sites.name: Nowa Huta
sites.description: A planned socialist-realist district built from 1949 around a vast steelworks, designed as a model communist town. Its monumental central square, broad avenues and uniform apartment blocks offer a striking contrast to medieval Kraków and a window into the People's Republic of Poland era.
sites.seasonal_notes: Accessible year-round by tram from the center. The wide, exposed avenues are best explored in mild weather; guided tours run throughout the year.
sites.booking_tips: Free to wander, though a guided tour, sometimes by vintage Trabant car, greatly enriches the visit. Reachable in about 30 minutes by tram from the Old Town.

sites.name: Zakrzówek
sites.description: A flooded former limestone quarry southwest of the center, famed for its striking turquoise water framed by white cliffs. Recently developed with boardwalks, beaches and viewpoints, it is a favorite spot for swimming in summer and scenic walks year-round.
sites.seasonal_notes: Most popular in summer when designated swimming areas open. The cliffs and turquoise water are photogenic in any season, though paths can be slippery in winter.
sites.booking_tips: Free to visit; swimming is allowed only in supervised, marked zones during the season. Reachable by tram and a short walk from the center.

sites.name: Tyniec Benedictine Abbey
sites.description: A Benedictine abbey founded in the 11th century, perched dramatically on a limestone bluff above the Vistula River southwest of Kraków. The monastery church, courtyards and museum reflect nearly a thousand years of monastic life, and the monks are known for their honey, herbal products and atmospheric setting.
sites.seasonal_notes: Open year-round, with concerts and special events more frequent in summer. The riverside cliff setting is beautiful in clear weather across all seasons.
sites.booking_tips: A small fee covers the museum and grounds; the church is open for services. Reach it by bus, by bike along the Vistula path, or by seasonal river cruise.

sites.name: Wieliczka Salt Mine
sites.description: A UNESCO-listed salt mine in operation from the 13th century until recent times, about 14 km southeast of Kraków. The visitor route winds through underground chambers, brine lakes and chapels carved entirely from rock salt, culminating in the breathtaking Chapel of St. Kinga with its salt chandeliers, altars and reliefs.
sites.seasonal_notes: Underground temperatures stay cool and constant year-round, making it ideal in any weather; bring a light layer. Summer and holiday periods are very busy.
sites.booking_tips: Book guided-tour tickets online in advance, as visits are by timed group only and sell out. The Tourist Route involves around 800 steps and roughly two hours underground.
